   679→   679→2
   680→   680→Related Work
   681→   681→One line of work focuses on incorporating numeri-
   682→   682→cal values directly into token representations, pro-
   683→   683→viding LMs with a prior. Charton (2022) explores
   684→   684→different number encodings based on scientific no-
   685→   685→tation for training LM solvers of linear algebra
   686→   686→problems. Golkar et al. (2023) propose represent-
   687→   687→ing numbers as a learned <NUM> token scaled
   688→   688→by the number scalar value, demonstrating how
   689→   689→models can adopt this scheme for regression tasks.
   690→   690→Another line of work investigates how models
   691→   691→learn to represent and process numerical informa-
   692→   692→tion. Nanda et al. (2023) show that a transformer
   693→   693→with one-hot encoding trained from scratch on mod-
   694→   694→ular addition discovers Fourier basis and its com-
   695→   695→putation is interpretable in trigonometric functions.
   696→   696→Kantamneni and Tegmark (2025) discover an anal-
   697→   697→ogous circuitry for (non-modular) addition in a
   698→   698→general pretrained language model, and find that its
   699→   699→intermediate representations combine both linear
   700→   700→and periodic components, reminiscent of a helix
   701→   701→structure. Zhou et al. (2024) further identifies sub-
   702→   702→components of the addition circuitry implemented
   703→   703→by the attention mechanism and feedforward layers.
   704→   704→Zhu et al. (2025) demonstrate that hidden states of
   705→   705→pretrained language models can be approximately
   706→   706→decoded with a linear (or multi-layer) probe to esti-
   707→   707→mate the logarithm of the number value. Although
   708→   708→the probe outputs correlate with the target value,
   709→   709→decoding achieves low accuracy. Recently, Levy
   710→   710→and Geva (2025) show success in recovering the
   711→   711→values of digits from internal representations of in-
   712→   712→termediate layers, hinting on a more generalized,
   713→   713→circular pattern in representations of numbers.
   714→   714→In summary, prior works suggest that language
   715→   715→models attempt to encode numerical information
   716→   716→into token representations during pretraining, but
   717→   717→their precision is rather limited. However, we hy-
   718→   718→pothesize that this perception stems from inade-
   719→   719→quate probing methods, and learned representations
   720→   720→are much more precise than previously estimated.
   721→   721→3
   722→   722→Recovering numerical information from
   723→   723→number embeddings
   724→   724→We study LMs from the Llama 3 (Grattafiori et al.,
   725→   725→2024), Phi 4 (Abdin et al., 2024), and OLMo 2
   726→   726→(OLMo et al., 2025) series, ranging from 1B to
   727→   727→72B parameters. Wide selection allows us to verify
   728→   728→the validity of our observations across a panel of
   729→   729→models sharing the characteristic of representing
   730→   730→all integers between 0 and 999 with unique tokens.
   731→   731→Motivations.
   732→   732→The central and foremost point to
   733→   733→address is whether the embeddings representing
   734→   734→specific numbers in LMs contain the numeric in-
   735→   735→formation of the value they represent. In practice,
   736→   736→this is best addressed with a probing setup: If em-
   737→   737→beddings do contain numerical information, we
   738→   738→should be able to learn a decoding function from
   739→   739→number embedding to the corresponding integer
   740→   740→value. Probing as a methodology comes with its
   741→   741→own set of caveats: probes should be kept as sim-
   742→   742→ple as possible, and their expressivity should be
   743→   743→compared against baseline benchmarks (Hewitt and
   744→   744→Liang, 2019). Our specific use case adds further
   745→   745→constraints: in particular, we have only one instance
   746→   746→per LLM of each integer representation, viz., there
   747→   747→is only one vector for the token 42. This rules out
   748→   748→naive classifier implementations, as we aim for the
   749→   749→probe to generalize to entirely unseen classes.

   795→   795→I.e., the ith row of B corresponds to the integer
   796→   796→i expressed in binary, whereas S is defined as a

   803→   803→dimensional space, a fixed matrix (S or B) allow-
   804→   804→ing us to encode integers using an a priori scheme,
   805→   805→and a learned projection Wout : Rd →Rh map-
   806→   806→ping these a priori representations onto the same
   807→   807→space as the reduced embeddings. Intuitively, Win
   808→   808→uncovers the underlying hidden structure of the

   816→   816→Implementation.
   817→   817→We evaluate the probes in
   818→   818→Equations (1) to (4) using a cross-validation setup
   819→   819→with 20 folds. We report their accuracy measured
   820→   820→by rounding the output of the regression probes
   821→   821→Equations (1) and (2) to the nearest integer, or
   822→   822→by retrieving the index of the row in S or B that
   823→   823→maximizes the output distribution of the classifier
   824→   824→probes Equations (3) and (4). We control the va-
   825→   825→lidity of our probes by ensuring that they reach
   826→   826→an accuracy of 0 for standard Gaussian vectors as
   827→   827→well as for a random permutation of the embed-
   828→   828→dings. Parameters for regressions are estimated
   829→   829→using a least-squares algorithm; whereas our clas-
   830→   830→sifiers’ parameters are optimized with Adam with
   831→   831→a learning rate of 0.0001, weight decay of 0.001,
   832→   832→and β = (0.9, 0.999). We choose a hidden di-
   833→   833→mension of 100. The classifiers are optimized to
   834→   834→distinguish output only between training tokens,
   835→   835→and during testing, must choose between all tokens.
   836→   836→The probes are optimized until loss converges on a
   837→   837→validation split separate from the testing split.
   838→   838→We release an implementation and training
   839→   839→recipes for the new probes, including all configura-
   840→   840→tions we use, in the project’s GitHub repository.1

   896→   896→Results.
   897→   897→We summarize performances, measured
   898→   898→in terms of accuracy, in Figure 1. Crucially, we are
   899→   899→almost systematically able to retrieve the integer
   900→   900→value corresponding to the embedding’s number
   901→   901→with very high accuracy. Another salient observa-
   902→   902→tion is that fsin consistently outperforms all other
   903→   903→probe architectures including the regression probe
   904→   904→used in previous work of Zhu et al. (2025), con-
   905→   905→tradicting their finding that LMs learn to encode
   906→   906→numbers linearly. Explaining the success of the
   907→   907→Fourier basis, we note that other prior literature
   908→   908→1https://github.com/prompteus/numllama
   909→   909→has suggested that sinusoidal features are used for
   910→   910→arithmetic computation in LMs (Zhou et al., 2024).
   911→   911→Adding onto this, we can also stress that, qualita-
   912→   912→tively, most of the models’ whose number embed-
   913→   913→dings we survey here exhibit wave-like patterns in
   914→   914→a PCA projection and have sparse Fourier trans-
   915→   915→form, confirming regularity in the hidden structure.
   916→   916→See Figures 2 and 3 in Section A.1 for visualiza-
   917→   917→tions of PCA and its Fourier transform. Notably,
   918→   918→OLMo 2 32B is the only model with low resem-
   919→   919→blance of the pattern, which is consistent with the
   920→   920→low performance of its sinusoidal probe.
   921→   921→Analysis.
   922→   922→To verify that our sin-base probes in-
   923→   923→deed reach their superior accuracy by learning to
   924→   924→extract a generalized, sin-like representation from
   925→   925→models’ representations, we analyse the encoded
   926→   926→representations that trained sin probes produce as
   927→   927→the output of Win. We experiment with two train-
   928→   928→ing settings: (i) using L1 regularization — encour-
   929→   929→aging sparsity, and (ii) using L2 regularization —
   930→   930→encouraging the employment of a broader scale
   931→   931→of input features. We note that in both of these
   932→   932→settings, the probes achieve almost identical gener-
   933→   933→alization capacity as assessed by their accuracy on
   934→   934→unseen inputs (embeddings of numbers).
   935→   935→Figure 6 in Appendix A.3 displays the resulting
   936→   936→representations for model embeddings of Llama3
   937→   937→1B associated with different numeric values. We
   938→   938→can observe that the L1- and L2-regularized probes
   939→   939→learn a substantially distinct representational pat-
   940→   940→tern. We hypothesize that a main difference be-
   941→   941→tween probes trained with different regularizations
   942→   942→is that the L1 probe learns to follow a broader scale
   943→   943→of distinct frequencies, while the L2 probe follows
   944→   944→similar frequencies shifted by a different constant.
   945→   945→Nevertheless, in both of the cases, the probe learns
   946→   946→a projection into a wave-like pattern across input
   947→   947→numbers, thus successfully following their injected
   948→   948→inductive bias.
   949→   949→4
   950→   950→Leveraging numerical information from
   951→   951→number embeddings
   952→   952→Motivation.
   953→   953→Having established that number em-
   954→   954→beddings do encode retrieval numerical informa-
   955→   955→tion about the integers they represent, we now turn
   956→   956→to how this numerical information is leveraged
   957→   957→by LMs to perform arithmetic tasks. We study the
   958→   958→zero-shot performances of a subset of our models
   959→   959→on addition and subtraction tasks. We define our
   960→   960→addition task as taking any pair of integers x1, x2
   961→   961→such that 0 < xi < 500 as input, and computing

   972→   972→Do NLP Models Know Numbers? Probing Numeracy in Embeddings
   973→   973→Eric Wallace∗1, Yizhong Wang∗2, Sujian Li2, Sameer Singh3, Matt Gardner1
   974→   974→1Allen Institute for Artiﬁcial Intelligence
   975→   975→2Peking University
   976→   976→3University of California, Irvine
   977→   977→{ericw, mattg}@allenai.org, {yizhong, lisujian}@pku.edu.cn, sameer@uci.edu
   978→   978→Abstract
   979→   979→The ability to understand and work with num-
   980→   980→bers (numeracy) is critical for many complex
   981→   981→reasoning tasks. Currently, most NLP models
   982→   982→treat numbers in text in the same way as other
   983→   983→tokens—they embed them as distributed vec-
   984→   984→tors. Is this enough to capture numeracy? We
   985→   985→begin by investigating the numerical reason-
   986→   986→ing capabilities of a state-of-the-art question
   987→   987→answering model on the DROP dataset. We
   988→   988→ﬁnd this model excels on questions that require
   989→   989→numerical reasoning, i.e., it already captures
   990→   990→numeracy.
   991→   991→To understand how this capabil-
   992→   992→ity emerges, we probe token embedding meth-
   993→   993→ods (e.g., BERT, GloVe) on synthetic list max-
   994→   994→imum, number decoding, and addition tasks.
   995→   995→A surprising degree of numeracy is naturally
   996→   996→present in standard embeddings.
   997→   997→For exam-
   998→   998→ple, GloVe and word2vec accurately encode
   999→   999→magnitude for numbers up to 1,000.
  1000→  1000→Fur-
  1001→  1001→thermore, character-level embeddings are even
  1002→  1002→more precise—ELMo captures numeracy the
  1003→  1003→best for all pre-trained methods—but BERT,
  1004→  1004→which uses sub-word units, is less exact.
  1005→  1005→1
  1006→  1006→Introduction
  1007→  1007→Neural NLP models have become the de-facto
  1008→  1008→standard tool across language understanding tasks,
  1009→  1009→even solving basic reading comprehension and
  1010→  1010→textual entailment datasets (Yu et al., 2018; Devlin
  1011→  1011→et al., 2019). Despite this, existing models are in-
  1012→  1012→capable of complex forms of reasoning, in partic-
  1013→  1013→ular, we focus on the ability to reason numerically.
  1014→  1014→Recent datasets such as DROP (Dua et al., 2019),
  1015→  1015→EQUATE (Ravichander et al., 2019), or Mathe-
  1016→  1016→matics Questions (Saxton et al., 2019) test numer-
  1017→  1017→ical reasoning; they contain examples which re-
  1018→  1018→quire comparing, sorting, and adding numbers in
  1019→  1019→natural language (e.g., Figure 2).
  1020→  1020→The ﬁrst step in performing numerical reason-
  1021→  1021→ing over natural language is numeracy: the abil-

  1056→  1056→the model’s predictions for all numbers from [-2000,
  1057→  1057→2000]. The model accurately decodes numbers within
  1058→  1058→the training range (in blue), i.e., pre-trained embed-

  1063→  1063→ity to understand and work with numbers in ei-
  1064→  1064→ther digit or word form (Spithourakis and Riedel,
  1065→  1065→2018). For example, one must understand that the
  1066→  1066→string “23” represents a bigger value than “twenty-
  1067→  1067→two”. Once a number’s value is (perhaps implic-
  1068→  1068→itly) represented, reasoning algorithms can then
  1069→  1069→process the text, e.g., extracting the list of ﬁeld
  1070→  1070→goals and computing that list’s maximum (ﬁrst
  1071→  1071→question in Figure 2). Learning to reason numer-
  1072→  1072→ically over paragraphs with only question-answer
  1073→  1073→supervision appears daunting for end-to-end mod-
  1074→  1074→els; our work seeks to understand if and how “out-
  1075→  1075→of-the-box” neural NLP models already learn this.
  1076→  1076→We begin by analyzing the state-of-the-art
  1077→  1077→NAQANet model (Dua et al., 2019) for DROP—
  1078→  1078→testing it on a subset of questions that evaluate nu-
  1079→  1079→arXiv:1909.07940v2  [cs.CL]  18 Sep 2019
  1080→  1080→
  1081→  1081→
  1082→  1082→--- PAGE 2 ---
  1083→  1083→merical reasoning (Section 2).
  1084→  1084→To our surprise,
  1085→  1085→the model exhibits excellent numerical reasoning
  1086→  1086→abilities. Amidst reading and comprehending nat-
  1087→  1087→ural language, the model successfully computes
  1088→  1088→list maximums/minimums, extracts superlative en-
  1089→  1089→tities (argmax reasoning), and compares numer-
  1090→  1090→ical quantities. For instance, despite NAQANet
  1091→  1091→achieving only 49 F1 on the entire validation set, it
  1092→  1092→scores 89 F1 on numerical comparison questions.
  1093→  1093→We also stress test the model by perturbing the val-
  1094→  1094→idation paragraphs and ﬁnd one failure mode: the
  1095→  1095→model struggles to extrapolate to numbers outside
  1096→  1096→its training range.
  1097→  1097→We are especially intrigued by the model’s abil-
  1098→  1098→ity to learn numeracy, i.e., how does the model
  1099→  1099→know the value of a number given its embedding?
  1100→  1100→The model uses standard embeddings (GloVe and
  1101→  1101→a Char-CNN) and receives no direct supervision
  1102→  1102→for number magnitude/ordering.
  1103→  1103→To understand
  1104→  1104→how numeracy emerges, we probe token embed-
  1105→  1105→ding methods (e.g., BERT, GloVe) using synthetic
  1106→  1106→list maximum, number decoding, and addition
  1107→  1107→tasks (Section 3).
  1108→  1108→We ﬁnd that all widely-used pre-trained embed-
  1109→  1109→dings, e.g., ELMo (Peters et al., 2018), BERT (De-
  1110→  1110→vlin et al., 2019), and GloVe (Pennington et al.,
  1111→  1111→2014), capture numeracy: number magnitude is
  1112→  1112→present in the embeddings, even for numbers in
  1113→  1113→the thousands. Among all embeddings, character-
  1114→  1114→level methods exhibit stronger numeracy than
  1115→  1115→word- and sub-word-level methods (e.g., ELMo
  1116→  1116→excels while BERT struggles), and character-level
  1117→  1117→models learned directly on the synthetic tasks are
  1118→  1118→the strongest overall. Finally, we investigate why
  1119→  1119→NAQANet had trouble extrapolating—was it a
  1120→  1120→failure in the model or the embeddings? We repeat
  1121→  1121→our probing tasks and test for model extrapolation,
  1122→  1122→ﬁnding that neural models struggle to predict num-
  1123→  1123→bers outside the training range.
  1124→  1124→2
  1125→  1125→Numeracy Case Study: DROP QA
  1126→  1126→This section examines the state-of-the-art model
  1127→  1127→for DROP by investigating its accuracy on ques-
  1128→  1128→tions that require numerical reasoning.
  1129→  1129→2.1
  1130→  1130→DROP Dataset
  1131→  1131→DROP is a reading comprehension dataset that
  1132→  1132→tests numerical reasoning operations such as
  1133→  1133→counting, sorting, and addition (Dua et al., 2019).
  1134→  1134→The dataset’s input-output format is a superset
  1135→  1135→of SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016):
  1136→  1136→the an-
  1137→  1137→. . . JaMarcus Russell completed a 91-yard touchdown pass
  1138→  1138→to rookie wide receiver Chaz Schilens. The Texans would
  1139→  1139→respond with fullback Vonta Leach getting a 1-yard touch-
  1140→  1140→down run, yet the Raiders would answer with kicker Se-
  1141→  1141→bastian Janikowski getting a 33-yard and a 21-yard ﬁeld
  1142→  1142→goal. Houston would tie the game in the second quarter
  1143→  1143→with kicker Kris Brown getting a 53-yard and a 24-yard
  1144→  1144→ﬁeld goal. Oakland would take the lead in the third quar-
  1145→  1145→ter with wide receiver Johnnie Lee Higgins catching a 29-
  1146→  1146→yard touchdown pass from Russell, followed up by an 80-
  1147→  1147→yard punt return for a touchdown.
  1148→  1148→Q: How many yards was the longest ﬁeld goal? A: 53
  1149→  1149→Q: How long was the shortest touchdown pass? A: 29-yard
  1150→  1150→Q: Who caught the longest touchdown? A: Chaz Schilens
  1151→  1151→Figure 2: Three DROP questions that require numer-
  1152→  1152→ical reasoning; the state-of-the-art NAQANet answers
  1153→  1153→every question correct. Plausible answer candidates to
  1154→  1154→the questions are underlined and the model’s predic-
  1155→  1155→tions are shown in bold.
  1156→  1156→swers are paragraph spans, as well as question
  1157→  1157→spans, number answers (e.g., 35), and dates (e.g.,
  1158→  1158→03/01/2014). The only supervision provided is the
  1159→  1159→question-answer pairs, i.e., a model must learn to
  1160→  1160→reason numerically while simultaneously learning
  1161→  1161→to read and comprehend.
  1162→  1162→2.2
  1163→  1163→NAQANet Model
  1164→  1164→Modeling approaches for DROP include both
  1165→  1165→semantic parsing (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017)
  1166→  1166→and reading comprehension (Yu et al., 2018)
  1167→  1167→models.
  1168→  1168→We focus on the latter, speciﬁcally
  1169→  1169→on Numerically-augmented QANet (NAQANet),
  1170→  1170→the current state-of-the-art model (Dua et al.,
  1171→  1171→2019).1 The model’s core structure closely follows
  1172→  1172→QANet (Yu et al., 2018) except that it contains four
  1173→  1173→output branches, one for each of the four answer
  1174→  1174→types (passage span, question span, count answer,
  1175→  1175→or addition/subtraction of numbers.)
  1176→  1176→Words and numbers are represented as the con-
  1177→  1177→catenation of GloVe embeddings and the output
  1178→  1178→of a character-level CNN. The model contains
  1179→  1179→no auxiliary components for representing number
  1180→  1180→magnitude or performing explicit comparisons.
  1181→  1181→We refer readers to Yu et al. (2018) and Dua et al.
  1182→  1182→(2019) for further details.

  1186→  1186→numeracy to answer, namely Comparative and

content.text:
Now I have all the content needed. Here is the analysis of each paper:

---

## Paper 1: Understanding Addition and Subtraction in Transformers

- **Title:** Understanding Addition and Subtraction in Transformers
- **Authors:** Philip Quirke (Martian), Clement Neo (NTU), Fazl Barez (University of Oxford, Martian)
- **Year:** 2024 (preprint, arXiv:2402.02619, updated October 2025)

**Key Contribution:** Demonstrates that small transformers (2-3 layers, 3-4 attention heads, up to 10M parameters) trained from scratch can solve n-digit addition and subtraction with 99.999% accuracy. Provides a unified mechanistic account based on cascading carry and borrow circuits, and releases an interpretability toolkit.

**Methodology Overview:**
- Constructed synthetic training data enriched with hardest edge cases (cascading carry/borrow).
- Trained 49 small transformer models on 5- to 12-digit arithmetic.
- Introduced mathematically exact left-to-right algorithms for addition and subtraction using features: single-digit carry-one values (ST_n), multidigit carry-one values (SV_n), base-add (SA_n).
- Validated learned mechanisms through systematic ablations and node-level constraints.
- Surveyed 180 publicly available LLMs for arithmetic capability.

**Datasets Used:** Synthetic (effectively infinite) training data for n-digit addition/subtraction; 180 public LLMs surveyed for benchmarking.

**Key Findings:**
- Small transformers converge rapidly to >99.999% accuracy on n-digit arithmetic.
- All accurate models converge on the same algorithmic solution with interpretable carry/borrow circuits.
- Only 7% of 180 surveyed LLMs can reliably perform addition; top performers rely on external tools.
- The same attention heads handle both addition and subtraction operations.

**Code Availability:** Yes -- `https://github.com/PhilipQuirke/quanta_mech_interp` (interpretability library) and `https://github.com/PhilipQuirke/quanta_maths` (project code).

**Relevance to Integer Interval Representation:** Highly relevant. Shows exactly how transformers implement integer arithmetic circuits with cascading mechanisms. The mechanistic interpretability approach (node search, ablation, constraint validation) provides a direct template for studying how transformers might learn to represent and reason about integer intervals/ranges. The carry-propagation mechanism is analogous to boundary-checking in interval tasks.

---

## Paper 2: LLMs Know More About Numbers than They Can Say

- **Title:** LLMs Know More About Numbers than They Can Say
- **Authors:** Fengting Yuchi (Shanghai Jiao Tong University), Li Du (Johns Hopkins University), Jason Eisner (Johns Hopkins University)
- **Year:** 2026 (arXiv:2602.07812, February 2026)

**Key Contribution:** Demonstrates that LLM hidden states linearly encode log-magnitudes of numbers (recoverable via simple linear probes) with high accuracy, even though the models cannot reliably verbalize this knowledge for cross-notation comparisons. Shows that incorporating probe loss as an auxiliary finetuning objective improves verbalized accuracy.

**Methodology Overview:**
- Trained linear regression probes on hidden states of 7B-8B LLMs to predict log2 magnitudes of numerals.
- Trained linear classification probes (logistic regression) on hidden states to predict pairwise numerical rankings.
- Tested on cross-notation comparisons (e.g., scientific notation vs. decimal).
- Finetuned models with auxiliary probing loss (L_total = L_LM + beta * L_cls).
- Evaluated 7 open-source models plus GPT-4.1 and GPT-4.1-mini.

**Datasets Used:** Synthetic cross-notation dataset (pairs of numbers in scientific vs. decimal notation); arXiv dataset derived from peS2o (Soldaini and Lo, 2023) for real-world scientific text.

**Key Findings:**
- Linear probes recover log-magnitudes with ~2.3% median relative error on synthetic data, ~19.06% on arXiv papers.
- Linear classifiers achieve >90% accuracy on pairwise comparison from hidden states, while verbalized accuracy is only 50-70%.
- Early-layer probe accuracy predicts verbalized performance across models.
- Auxiliary probe loss during finetuning yields an additional 3.22% improvement in verbalized accuracy.

**Code Availability:** Yes -- `github.com/VCY019/Numeracy-Probing`

**Relevance to Integer Interval Representation:** Very relevant. The linear encoding of log-magnitudes in hidden states is directly pertinent to understanding how transformers internally represent numerical values. The finding that internal representations are far more accurate than verbalized outputs suggests that transformers may encode interval boundary information precisely but struggle to express it. The probing methodology (linear regression/classification on hidden states) can be directly adapted for studying interval membership representations.

---

## Paper 3: Pre-trained Language Models Learn Remarkably Accurate Representations of Numbers

- **Title:** Pre-trained Language Models Learn Remarkably Accurate Representations of Numbers
- **Authors:** Marek Kadlcik, Michal Stefanik, Timothee Mickus, Michal Spiegel, Josef Kuchar (Masaryk University, University of Helsinki, Kempelen Institute)
- **Year:** 2025 (arXiv:2506.08966, June/October 2025)

**Key Contribution:** Proposes a novel sinusoidal probing technique that decodes numeric values from input embeddings with near-perfect accuracy (up to 100% for several models). Challenges prior work claiming LM number representations are imprecise, showing the issue was inadequate probing methods rather than inadequate representations.

**Methodology Overview:**
- Designed four probe architectures: f_lin (linear), f_log_lin (log-linear), f_sin (sinusoidal/Fourier basis), f_bin (binary basis).
- The sinusoidal probe uses a fixed Fourier basis matrix S with sin/cos components, combined with learned input/output projections.
- Evaluated on integer token embeddings (0-999) across Llama 3 (1B-70B), Phi 4 (15B), and OLMo 2 (1B-32B) series.
- Used 20-fold cross-validation; validated with Gaussian random and permutation baselines.
- Demonstrated that probe accuracy correlates with arithmetic task performance, and that aligning embeddings with the sinusoidal pattern improves arithmetic.

**Datasets Used:** Token embeddings for integers 0-999 from multiple LM families; addition and subtraction tasks with pairs of integers x1, x2 where 0 < x_i < 500.

**Key Findings:**
- Sinusoidal probes (f_sin) achieve near-perfect accuracy (94-100%) on most models, dramatically outperforming linear probes used in prior work.
- Number embeddings exhibit wave-like (sinusoidal) patterns visible in PCA projections with sparse Fourier transforms.
- OLMo 2 32B is an outlier -- does not use sinusoidal pattern yet still performs well on arithmetic.
- Embedding precision (measured by probe accuracy) explains a large portion of LM arithmetic errors.
- Intervening on embeddings to better follow the sinusoidal pattern improves arithmetic performance.

**Code Availability:** Yes -- `https://github.com/prompteus/numllama`

**Relevance to Integer Interval Representation:** Critically relevant. The sinusoidal/Fourier structure of number embeddings is fundamental to understanding how transformers represent integers. If integer values are encoded via periodic basis functions, then interval membership (is x in [a,b]?) could potentially be computed through simple operations on these sinusoidal representations. The probe architecture (learned projection into Fourier space) provides a concrete tool for investigating interval boundary encoding. The finding that embedding precision predicts arithmetic accuracy suggests that interval accuracy would similarly depend on embedding quality.

---

## Paper 4: Do NLP Models Know Numbers? Probing Numeracy in Embeddings

- **Title:** Do NLP Models Know Numbers? Probing Numeracy in Embeddings
- **Authors:** Eric Wallace (AI2), Yizhong Wang (Peking University), Sujian Li (Peking University), Sameer Singh (UC Irvine), Matt Gardner (AI2)
- **Year:** 2019 (arXiv:1909.07940, September 2019)

**Key Contribution:** The foundational/seminal work on probing numeracy in NLP embeddings. Shows that standard embeddings (GloVe, word2vec, ELMo, BERT) naturally encode number magnitude, with character-level methods (ELMo) being most precise. Also demonstrates a key failure mode: neural models struggle to extrapolate to numbers outside the training range.

**Methodology Overview:**
- Probed token embeddings on three synthetic tasks: list maximum, number decoding (embedding to scalar), and addition.
- Trained probing models to decode numbers from their embeddings over integers in [-500, 500].
- Tested extrapolation to [-2000, 2000].
- Analyzed NAQANet (state-of-the-art on DROP) for numerical reasoning on comparative and superlative questions.
- Evaluated GloVe, word2vec, ELMo, BERT, Char-CNN, and Char-LSTM embeddings.

**Datasets Used:** DROP dataset (reading comprehension with numerical reasoning); synthetic tasks (list maximum, number decoding, addition) over integer ranges.

**Key Findings:**
- All standard pre-trained embeddings capture numeracy: number magnitude is encoded even for numbers in the thousands.
- Character-level embeddings (ELMo) are most precise; BERT (sub-word) is less exact.
- NAQANet achieves 89 F1 on binary comparison questions (40 points above average), showing emergent numeracy.
- Critical failure mode: models cannot extrapolate to numbers outside the training range -- predictions saturate or diverge.
- Jointly-trained character-level models (Char-CNN, Char-LSTM) achieve strongest numeracy on synthetic tasks.

**Code Availability:** Not explicitly mentioned in the first 3 pages (but the paper is from AI2 and code was later made available).

**Relevance to Integer Interval Representation:** Foundational relevance. This is the seminal paper establishing that embeddings encode numerical magnitude, which is a prerequisite for interval reasoning. The extrapolation failure finding is directly relevant: if transformers cannot represent numbers outside their training range, they will fail on intervals involving such numbers. The probing methodology (number decoding from embeddings) is the ancestor of all subsequent probing approaches used in the other three papers. The list maximum and comparison tasks are closely related to interval boundary operations.

python3 << 'PYEOF'
import fitz
import os

papers = [
    "chen2024_states_hidden_in_hidden_states.pdf",
    "alquboj2025_number_representations_human_perception.pdf",
    "yu2024_arithmetic_neuron_analysis.pdf",
    "bertolazzi2025_validation_gap_arithmetic.pdf",
    "deng2024_symbolic_learners_arithmetic.pdf",
    "hasani2025_counting_llm_vlm.pdf",
    "chang2024_inductive_biases_counting.pdf",
    "zhang2024_counting_ability_tokenization.pdf",
    "fu2024_llms_count_letters.pdf",
    "rahman2025_fragile_number_sense.pdf",
    "gross2024_compact_proofs_mechanistic.pdf",
    "bereska2024_mechanistic_interpretability_review.pdf",
]

base = "/workspaces/transformer-int-intervals-claude/papers/"

for i, p in enumerate(papers, 1):
    path = os.path.join(base, p)
    print(f"\n{'='*80}")
    print(f"PAPER {i}: {p}")
    print('='*80)
    try:
        doc = fitz.open(path)
        page = doc[0]
        text = page.get_text()
        print(text[:3000])
        doc.close()
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"ERROR: {e}")
PYEOF

States Hidden in Hidden States:
LLMs Emerge Discrete State Representations Implicitly
Junhao Chen, Shengding Hu, Zhiyuan Liu∗, Maosong Sun∗
Department of Computer Science and Technology, Tsinghua University
Beijing, China
chenjunh22@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit vari-
ous emergent abilities. Among these abilities,
some might reveal the internal working mech-
anisms of models. In this paper, we uncover a
novel emergent capability in models: the intrin-
sic ability to perform extended sequences of cal-
culations without relying on chain-of-thought
step-by-step solutions. Remarkably, the most
advanced models can directly output the results
of two-digit number additions with lengths ex-
tending up to 15 addends. We hypothesize that
the model emerges Implicit Discrete State Rep-
resentations (IDSRs) within its hidden states
and performs symbolic calculations internally.
To test this hypothesis, we design a sequence
of experiments that look into the hidden states.
Specifically, we first confirm that IDSRs ex-
ist. Then, we provide interesting observations
about the formation of IDSRs from layer, digit,
and sequence perspectives. Finally, we confirm
that models indeed use IDSRs to produce the
final answers. However, we also discover that
these state representations are far from lossless
in current open-sourced models, leading to in-
accuracies in their final performance. Our work
presents a novel exploration of LLMs’ sym-
bolic calculation abilities and the underlying
mechanisms.

    77→Number Representations in LLMs:
    78→A Computational Parallel to Human Perception
    79→H. V. AlquBoj∗
    80→Hilal AlQuabeh∗1
    81→Velibor Bojkovic∗1

    90→Abstract
    91→Humans are believed to perceive numbers on a
    92→logarithmic mental number line, where smaller
    93→values are represented with greater resolution
    94→than larger ones. This cognitive bias, supported
    95→by neuroscience and behavioral studies, sug-
    96→gests that numerical magnitudes are processed
    97→in a sublinear fashion rather than on a uni-
    98→form linear scale. Inspired by this hypothe-
    99→sis, we investigate whether large language mod-
   100→els (LLMs) exhibit a similar logarithmic-like
   101→structure in their internal numerical represen-
   102→tations. By analyzing how numerical values
   103→are encoded across different layers of LLMs,
   104→we apply dimensionality reduction techniques
   105→such as PCA and PLS followed by geometric
   106→regression to uncover latent structures in the
   107→learned embeddings. Our findings reveal that
   108→the model’s numerical representations exhibit
   109→sublinear spacing, with distances between val-
   110→ues aligning with a logarithmic scale. This
   111→suggests that LLMs, much like humans, may
   112→encode numbers in a compressed, non-uniform
   113→manner12.

   153→PAPER 3: yu2024_arithmetic_neuron_analysis.pdf
   154→================================================================================
   155→Interpreting Arithmetic Mechanism in Large Language Models
   156→through Comparative Neuron Analysis
   157→Zeping Yu
   158→Sophia Ananiadou
   159→Department of Computer Science, The University of Manchester
   160→{zeping.yu@postgrad. sophia.ananiadou@}manchester.ac.uk
   161→Abstract
   162→We find arithmetic ability resides within a lim-
   163→ited number of attention heads, with each head
   164→specializing in distinct operations. To delve
   165→into the reason, we introduce the Comparative
   166→Neuron Analysis (CNA) method, which iden-
   167→tifies an internal logic chain consisting of four
   168→distinct stages from input to prediction: feature
   169→enhancing with shallow FFN neurons, feature
   170→transferring by shallow attention layers, fea-
   171→ture predicting by arithmetic heads, and pre-
   172→diction enhancing among deep FFN neurons.
   173→Moreover, we identify the human-interpretable
   174→FFN neurons within both feature-enhancing
   175→and feature-predicting stages. These findings
   176→lead us to investigate the mechanism of LoRA,
   177→revealing that it enhances prediction probabil-
   178→ities by amplifying the coefficient scores of
   179→FFN neurons related to predictions. Finally, we
   180→apply our method in model pruning for arith-
   181→metic tasks and model editing for reducing gen-
   182→der bias. Code is on https://github.com/
   183→zepingyu0512/arithmetic-mechanism.
   184→1
   185→Introduction
   186→Arithmetic ability is a crucial foundational skill
   187→of large language models (LLMs) (Brown et al.,
   188→2020; Ouyang et al., 2022; Chowdhery et al., 2023),
   189→contributing significantly to reasoning (Wei et al.,
   190→2022; Kojima et al., 2022) and mathematical tasks
   191→(Peng et al., 2021; Azerbayev et al., 2023). While
   192→existing studies (Quirke et al., 2023; Zhang et al.,
   193→2023; Stolfo et al., 2023) have made significant
   194→breakthroughs in understanding arithmetic tasks,
   195→the exact mechanism still remains elusive. Zhang
   196→et al. (2023) find that only a few attention heads sig-
   197→nificantly impact arithmetic performance, but they
   198→do not elaborate on the mechanisms of these heads
   199→or how they influence FFN layers. Stolfo et al.
   200→(2023) intervene the hidden states and find the infor-
   201→mation flow from number and operation positions
   202→to the last position. However, they do not locate the
   203→important attention heads (proved to store different
   204→abilities (Olsson et al., 2022; Gould et al., 2023))
   205→and FFN neurons (proved to store knowledge (Dai
   206→et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2022a)). Despite the
   207→challenge of pinpointing important FFN neurons
   208→among tens of thousands of nodes, many studies
   209→(Gurnee et al., 2023; Lieberum et al., 2023; Nanda
   210→et al., 2023) emphasize that considering FFN neu-
   211→rons as fundamental units is crucial for better un-
   212→derstanding FFN layers. Furthermore, as model
   213→editing typically occurs at the neuron level (Dai
   214→et al., 2021; Geva et al., 2022), it remains unclear
   215→how to effectively leverage the explanations due to
   216→the uncertainty surrounding the precise locations
   217→of important parameters.

   238→================================================================================
   239→The Validation Gap: A Mechanistic Analysis of How Language Models
   240→Compute Arithmetic but Fail to Validate It
   241→Leonardo Bertolazzi*1
   242→Philipp Mondorf*2, 3
   243→Barbara Plank2, 3
   244→Raffaella Bernardi4
   245→1DISI, University of Trento, Italy
   246→2MaiNLP, Center for Information and Language Processing, LMU Munich, Germany
   247→3Munich Center for Machine Learning (MCML), Munich, Germany
   248→4Free University of Bozen-Bolzano, Italy
   249→leonardo.bertolazzi@unitn.it
   250→{p.mondorf, b.plank}@lmu.de
   251→raffaella.bernardi@unibz.it
   252→Abstract
   253→The ability of large language models (LLMs)
   254→to validate their output and identify potential
   255→errors is crucial for ensuring robustness and re-
   256→liability. However, current research indicates
   257→that LLMs struggle with self-correction, en-
   258→countering significant challenges in detecting
   259→errors. While studies have explored methods
   260→to enhance self-correction in LLMs, relatively
   261→little attention has been given to understand-
   262→ing the models’ internal mechanisms underly-
   263→ing error detection. In this paper, we present
   264→a mechanistic analysis of error detection in
   265→LLMs, focusing on simple arithmetic prob-
   266→lems. Through circuit analysis, we identify the
   267→computational subgraphs responsible for detect-
   268→ing arithmetic errors across four smaller-sized
   269→LLMs. Our findings reveal that all models heav-
   270→ily rely on consistency heads—attention heads
   271→that assess surface-level alignment of numer-
   272→ical values in arithmetic solutions. Moreover,
   273→we observe that the models’ internal arithmetic
   274→computation primarily occurs in higher layers,
   275→whereas validation takes place in middle lay-
   276→ers, before the final arithmetic results are fully
   277→encoded. This structural dissociation between
   278→arithmetic computation and validation seems
   279→to explain why smaller-sized LLMs struggle to
   280→detect even simple arithmetic errors.
   281→1
   282→Introduction
   283→In recent years, large language models have demon-
   284→strated notable performance across a variety of
   285→reasoning tasks, including arithmetic problem-
   286→solving (Sawada et al., 2023; Phan et al., 2025;
   287→Liu et al., 2024a; Achiam et al., 2023). However,
   288→a gap appears to exist between the models’ ability
   289→to generate solutions and their capacity to validate
   290→them effectively (Huang et al., 2024; Hong et al.,
   291→2024; Jiang et al., 2024). Specifically, while LLMs
   292→are often able to correct mistakes once they have

   334→PAPER 5: deng2024_symbolic_learners_arithmetic.pdf
   335→================================================================================
   336→Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (01/2026)
   337→Language Models are Symbolic Learners in Arithmetic
   338→Chunyuan Deng
   339→chunyuan.deng@rice.edu
   340→Department of Computer Science
   341→Rice University
   342→Zhiqi Li
   343→zli3167@gatech.edu
   344→College of Computing
   345→Georgia Institute of Technology
   346→Roy Xie
   347→ruoyu.xie@duke.edu
   348→Department of Computer Science
   349→Duke University
   350→Ruidi Chang
   351→rc151@rice.edu
   352→Department of Computer Science
   353→Rice University
   354→Hanjie Chen
   355→hanjie@rice.edu
   356→Department of Computer Science
   357→Rice University
   358→Reviewed on OpenReview: https: // openreview. net/ forum? id= QSblPg1xUM
   359→Abstract
   360→The prevailing question in LM performing arithmetic is whether these models learn to truly
   361→compute or if they simply master superficial pattern matching. In this paper, we argues for
   362→the latter, presenting evidence that LMs act as greedy symbolic learners, prioritizing the
   363→simplest possible shortcuts to fit the stats of dataset to solve arithmetic tasks. To investigate
   364→this, we introduce subgroup induction, a practical framework adapted from Solomonoff
   365→Induction (SI), one of the most powerful universal predictors. Our framework analyzes
   366→arithmetic problems by breaking them down into “subgroups”—minimal mappings between
   367→a few input digits and a single output digit. Our primary metric, subgroup quality, measures
   368→the viability of these shortcuts. Experiments reveal a distinct U-shaped accuracy pattern in
   369→multi-digit multiplication: LMs quickly master the first and last output digits while struggling
   370→with those in the middle. We demonstrate this U-shape is not coincidental; it perfectly
   371→mirrors the quality of the simplest possible subgroups, those requiring the fewest input tokens.
   372→This alignment suggests a core learning mechanism: LMs first learn easy, low-token shortcuts
   373→and only incorporate more complex, multi-token patterns as training progresses. They do not
   374→learn the algorithm of multiplication but rather a hierarchy of increasingly complex symbol-
   375→to-symbol mappings. Ultimately, our findings suggest that the path to arithmetic mastery
   376→for LMs is not paved with algorithms, but with a cascade of simple, hierarchically-learned
   377→symbolic shortcuts. The code is at https://github.com/chili-lab/Symbolic-Arithmetic.
   378→Introduction
   379→The saturation of modern math benchmarks like GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) by frontier language models
   380→such as GPT-4o (OpenAI et al., 2024) and Claude (Anthropic, 2024) marks a significant milestone in AI. As

   382→more fundamental question: are these models developing genuine numerical reasoning, or are they exhibiting

   390→Understanding Counting Mechanisms in
   391→Large Language and Vision-Language Models
   392→Hosein Hasani* , Amirmohammad Izadi* , Fatemeh Askari* , Mobin Bagherian,
   393→Sadegh Mohammadian, Mohammad Izadi, and Mahdieh Soleymani Baghshah
   394→Department of Computer Engineering, Sharif University of Technology
   395→Abstract
   396→This paper examines how large language models (LLMs)
   397→and large vision-language models (LVLMs) represent and
   398→compute numerical information in counting tasks. We use
   399→controlled experiments with repeated textual and visual
   400→items and analyze model behavior through causal media-
   401→tion and activation patching. To this end, we design a spe-
   402→cialized tool, CountScope, for mechanistic interpretability

   404→or visual features encode latent positional count informa-
   405→tion that can be extracted and transferred across contexts.
   406→Layerwise analyses reveal a progressive emergence of nu-
   407→merical representations, with lower layers encoding small
   408→counts and higher layers representing larger ones. We iden-
   409→tify an internal counter mechanism that updates with each
   410→item, stored mainly in the final token or region and transfer-
   411→able between contexts. In LVLMs, numerical information
   412→also appears in visual embeddings, shifting between back-
   413→ground and foreground regions depending on spatial com-
   414→position. Models rely on structural cues such as separa-
   415→tors in text, which act as shortcuts for tracking item counts
   416→and influence the accuracy of numerical predictions. Over-
   417→all, counting emerges as a structured, layerwise process
   418→in LLMs and follows the same general pattern in LVLMs,
   419→shaped by the properties of the vision encoder.

   421→Counting is a fundamental operation that supports reason-

   452→PAPER 7: chang2024_inductive_biases_counting.pdf
   453→================================================================================
   454→Preprint, under review
   455→LANGUAGE MODELS NEED INDUCTIVE BIASES TO
   456→COUNT INDUCTIVELY
   457→Yingshan Chang & Yonatan Bisk
   458→Carnegie Mellon University
   459→{yingshac,ybisk}@cs.cmu.edu
   460→ABSTRACT
   461→Counting is a fundamental example of generalization, whether viewed through the
   462→mathematical lens of Peano’s axioms defining the natural numbers or the cognitive
   463→science literature for children learning to count. The argument holds for both cases
   464→that learning to count means learning to count infinitely. While few papers have
   465→tried to distill transformer "reasoning" to the simplest case of counting, investigating
   466→length generalization does occur throughout the literature. In the "train short, test
   467→long" paradigm of NLP, length refers to the training sentence length. In formal lan-
   468→guage recognition, length refers to the input sequence length, or the maximum stack
   469→size induced by a pushdown automata. In general problem solving, length refers
   470→to the number of hops in a deductive reasoning chain or the recursion depth. For
   471→all cases, counting is central to task success. And crucially, generalizing counting
   472→inductively is central to success on OOD instances. This work provides extensive
   473→empirical results on training language models to count. We experiment with archi-
   474→tectures ranging from RNNs, Transformers, State-Space Models and RWKV. We
   475→present carefully-designed task formats, auxiliary tasks and positional embeddings
   476→to avoid limitations in generalization with OOD-position and OOD-vocabulary. We
   477→find that while traditional RNNs trivially achieve inductive counting, Transformers
   478→have to rely on positional embeddings to count out-of-domain. As counting is
   479→the basis for many arguments concerning the expressivity of Transformers, our
   480→finding calls for the community to reexamine the application scope of primitive
   481→functions defined in formal characterizations. Finally, modern RNNs also largely
   482→underperform traditional RNNs in generalizing counting inductively. We discuss
   483→how design choices that enable parallelized training of modern RNNs cause them
   484→to lose merits of a recurrent nature.
   485→1
   486→INTRODUCTION
   487→“Difficulty in generalizing to longer instances” is a recurring theme in the discussion of Transformer
   488→limitations, regardless of the task domain (Dziri et al., 2023; Saparov et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023;
   489→Del’etang et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022; Bhattamishra et al., 2020). We find that, although the notion of
   490→length may vary across domains (e.g. sequence length, recursion depth, counter states for DSAs, stack
   491→sizes for PDAs), counting is always involved as a required component to successfully handle the task.
   492→In fact, counting might be leveraged by Transformers more often than necessary as it circumvents
   493→the need to implement recurrence. For example, Liu et al. (2022) indicates that Transformers may
   494→rely on internal representations of counts to model counter languages, as a remedy for its lack of a
   495→recurrent mechanism, but failing immediately on instances with OOD counts. Furthe

   498→PAPER 8: zhang2024_counting_ability_tokenization.pdf

   500→Counting Ability of Large Language Models and Impact of Tokenization
   501→Xiang Zhang1∗
   502→Juntai Cao1∗
   503→Chenyu You2
   504→1 University of British Columbia
   505→2 Stony Brook University
   506→xzhang23@ualberta.ca, jtcao7@cs.ubc.ca, chenyu.you@stonybrook.edu
   507→Abstract
   508→Transformers, the backbone of modern large
   509→language models (LLMs), face inherent archi-
   510→tectural limitations that impede their reasoning
   511→capabilities. Unlike recurrent networks, Trans-
   512→formers lack recurrent connections, confining
   513→them to constant-depth computation. This re-
   514→striction places them in the complexity class
   515→TC0, making them theoretically incapable of
   516→solving tasks that demand increasingly deep
   517→reasoning as input length grows. Counting,
   518→a fundamental component of many reasoning
   519→tasks, also requires reasoning depth to grow
   520→linearly to be performed inductively. While
   521→previous studies have established the upper lim-
   522→its of counting ability in Transformer-based ex-
   523→pert models (i.e., models specifically trained for
   524→counting tasks), these findings do not directly
   525→extend to general-purpose LLMs due to differ-
   526→ences in reasoning mechanisms. Recent work
   527→has highlighted how Chain of Thought (CoT)
   528→reasoning can help alleviate some of the archi-
   529→tectural limitations of Transformers in count-
   530→ing tasks. However, little attention has been
   531→paid to the role of tokenization in these models.
   532→Unlike expert models that often use character-
   533→level tokenization, LLMs typically rely on byte-
   534→level (BPE) tokenizers, which fundamentally
   535→alters the way reasoning is processed. Our
   536→work investigates the impact of tokenization
   537→on the counting abilities of LLMs, uncovering
   538→substantial performance variations based on in-
   539→put tokenization differences. We provide both
   540→theoretical and experimental analyses, offer-
   541→ing insights into how tokenization choices can
   542→undermine models’ theoretical computability,
   543→thereby inspiring the design of new tokeniza-
   544→tion methods to enhance reasoning in LLMs.

   574→Why Do Large Language Models (LLMs) Struggle to Count
   575→Letters?
   576→TAIRAN FU, College of Mechanical & Electrical Engineering, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and
   577→Astronautics, China
   578→RAQUEL FERRANDO, JAVIER CONDE, CARLOS ARRIAGA, and PEDRO REVIRIEGO, ETSI
   579→de Telecomunicación, Universidad Politécnica de Madrid, Spain
   580→Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved unprecedented performance on many complex tasks, being able,
   581→for example, to answer questions on almost any topic. However, they struggle with other simple tasks, such as
   582→counting the occurrences of letters in a word, as illustrated by the inability of many LLMs to count the number
   583→of "r" letters in "strawberry". Several works have studied this problem and linked it to the tokenization used by
   584→LLMs, to the intrinsic limitations of the attention mechanism, or to the lack of character-level training data. In
   585→this paper, we conduct an experimental study to evaluate the relations between the LLM errors when counting
   586→letters with 1) the frequency of the word and its components in the training dataset and 2) the complexity
   587→of the counting operation. We present a comprehensive analysis of the errors of LLMs when counting letter
   588→occurrences by evaluating a representative group of models over a large number of words. The results show a
   589→number of consistent trends in the models evaluated: 1) models are capable of recognizing the letters but not
   590→counting them; 2) the frequency of the word and tokens in the word does not have a significant impact on the
   591→LLM errors; 3) there is a positive correlation of letter frequency with errors, more frequent letters tend to
   592→have more counting errors, 4) the errors show a strong correlation with the number of letters or tokens in a
   593→word and 5) the strongest correlation occurs with the number of letters with counts larger than one, with
   594→most models being unable to correctly count words in which letters appear more than twice. These results
   595→suggest that the problems of LLMs to count letters are not related to the frequency of words or tokens in the
   596→training data but to the complexity of the counting operation. However, further studies are needed to build a
   597→better understanding of the limitations of LLMs to count the letters in a word.

   599→ologies →Natural language generation; Language resources.

   603→guage Models (LLMs) Struggle to Count Letters?. 1, 1 (December 2023), 16 pages. https://doi.org/XXXXXXX.
   604→XXXXXXX
   605→1
   606→INTRODUCTION
   607→Since the introduction of ChatGPT, the adoption of large language models (LLMs) has been exponen-
   608→tial and are used by millions of people [22]. This is because LLMs have achieved an unprecedented

   612→PAPER 10: rahman2025_fragile_number_sense.pdf
   613→================================================================================
   614→A Fragile Number Sense: Probing the Elemental
   615→Limits of Numerical Reasoning in LLMs
   616→Roussel Rahman
   617→Stanford University
   618→Stanford, CA 94305
   619→roussel.rahman@gmail.com
   620→Aashwin Ananda Mishra
   621→SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory
   622→Menlo Park, CA 94025
   623→aashwin@stanford.edu
   624→Abstract
   625→Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable emergent capa-
   626→bilities, yet the robustness of their numerical reasoning remains an open question.
   627→While standard benchmarks evaluate LLM reasoning on complex problem sets us-
   628→ing aggregated metrics, they often obscure foundational weaknesses. In this work,
   629→we probe LLM mathematical numeracy by evaluating performance on problems
   630→of escalating complexity, from constituent operations to combinatorial puzzles.
   631→We test several state-of-the-art LLM-based agents on a 100-problem challenge
   632→comprising four categories: (1) basic arithmetic, (2) advanced operations, (3) pri-
   633→mality checking, and (4) the Game of 24 number puzzle. Our results show that
   634→while the agents achieved high accuracy on the first three categories, which require
   635→deterministic algorithmic execution, they consistently failed at the number puzzle,
   636→underlining its demand for a heuristic search over a large combinatorial space to be
   637→a significant bottleneck. These findings reveal that the agents’ proficiency is largely
   638→confined to recalling and executing known algorithms, rather than performing gen-
   639→erative problem-solving. This suggests their apparent numerical reasoning is more
   640→akin to sophisticated pattern-matching than flexible, analytical thought, limiting
   641→their potential for tasks that require novel or creative numerical insights.
   642→Keywords: LLMs; LRMs; Numerical Reasoning; Problem Solving
   643→1
   644→Introduction
   645→LLMs have demonstrated exceptional abilities at diverse tasks, such as writing code, composing
   646→essays, and solving mathematical problems. A recent evolution is specifically designed for tasks
   647→involving reasoning, referred to as Large Reasoning Models (LRMs). However, most of their
   648→perceived reasoning abilities are examined using final performance on large benchmark problem sets,
   649→while the actual reasoning processes remain less explored.
   650→LLMs (and LRMs) reason through chains and trees of thoughts, guided by reinforcement learning
   651→from sources like human feedback or simulation-based optimization. To understand the reasoning
   652→processes, their constituent elements need to be investigated. Importantly, at each step of the chains or
   653→the trees, the models reason by probabilistic pattern matching and generate responses as probabilistic
   654→token predictions, each with less than 100% probability of success. Therefore, as the number of
   655→reasoning steps increases, the probability of accuracy drops exponentially. Moreover, if the LLMs are
   656→weak at one or a few specific steps, the weakness may propagate and cause the trace to diverge towards
   657→inaccurate solutions. Evaluations relying on large aggregate benchmarks would obscure this fragility
   658→of reasoning that may compromise the mo
   659→

   661→PAPER 11: gross2024_compact_proofs_mechanistic.pdf
   662→================================================================================
   663→Compact Proofs of Model Performance via
   664→Mechanistic Interpretability
   665→Jason Gross∗
   666→Rajashree Agrawal
   667→Thomas Kwa†
   668→Euan Ong†
   669→Chun Hei Yip†
   670→Alex Gibson‡
   671→Soufiane Noubir‡
   672→Lawrence Chan
   673→Abstract
   674→We propose using mechanistic interpretability – techniques for reverse engineering
   675→model weights into human-interpretable algorithms – to derive and compactly
   676→prove formal guarantees on model performance. We prototype this approach by
   677→formally proving accuracy lower bounds for a small transformer trained on Max-of-
   678→K, validating proof transferability across 151 random seeds and four values of K.
   679→We create 102 different computer-assisted proof strategies and assess their length
   680→and tightness of bound on each of our models. Using quantitative metrics, we find
   681→that shorter proofs seem to require and provide more mechanistic understanding.
   682→Moreover, we find that more faithful mechanistic understanding leads to tighter
   683→performance bounds. We confirm these connections by qualitatively examining a
   684→subset of our proofs. Finally, we identify compounding structureless errors as a
   685→key challenge for using mechanistic interpretability to generate compact proofs on
   686→model performance.
   687→1
   688→Introduction
   689→One approach to ensuring the safety and reliability of powerful AI systems is via formally verified
   690→proofs of model performance [48, 11]. If we hope to deploy formal verification on increasingly
   691→large models [24, 27] with powerful emergent capabilities [56] across more diverse and broader
   692→domains [5, 46], we will need compact proofs of generalization bounds on specific models that certify
   693→global robustness. However, existing approaches tend to use proof strategies that suffer from bad
   694→asymptotic complexity, while verifying either generalization properties of training procedures or local
   695→robustness properties of specific models.
   696→One key challenge to verification is that neural network architectures are highly expressive [51, 58],
   697→and models with similar training procedure and performance may still have learned significantly
   698→different weights [38, 9]. This expressivity makes it difficult to adequately compress explanations of
   699→global model behavior in ways that correspond closely enough to the model’s actual mechanisms to
   700→be useful for efficient verification without being too lossy, especially when using only knowledge of
   701→the architecture or training procedure. We propose verifying model performance using understanding
   702→derived from mechanistic interpretability (Section 2) – that is, reverse engineering the specific
   703→implementation of the algorithm from the learned weights of particular models. Knowledge of the
   704→specific implementation allows us to construct less lossy simplifications of the model, and more
   705→efficiently reason about model performance over possible inputs.
   706→In this work, we provide a case study of translating mechanistic interpretations into compact proofs.
   707→We train an attention-only transformer on a Max-of-K task with 151 random seeds (Section 3), and
   708→∗Corresponding autho
   709→
   710→================================================================================
   711→PAPER 12: bereska2024_mechanistic_interpretability_review.pdf
   712→================================================================================
   713→Under review as submission to TMLR
   714→Mechanistic Interpretability for AI Safety
   715→A Review
   716→Leonard Bereska
   717→Efstratios Gavves
   718→{leonard.bereska, egavves}@uva.nl
   719→University of Amsterdam
   720→Abstract
   721→Understanding AI systems’ inner workings is critical for ensuring value alignment and safety.
   722→This review explores mechanistic interpretability: reverse engineering the computational
   723→mechanisms and representations learned by neural networks into human-understandable
   724→algorithms and concepts to provide a granular, causal understanding. We establish founda-
   725→tional concepts such as features encoding knowledge within neural activations and hypothe-
   726→ses about their representation and computation.
   727→We survey methodologies for causally
   728→dissecting model behaviors and assess the relevance of mechanistic interpretability to AI
   729→safety. We examine benefits in understanding, control, alignment, and risks such as ca-
   730→pability gains and dual-use concerns.
   731→We investigate challenges surrounding scalability,
   732→automation, and comprehensive interpretation. We advocate for clarifying concepts, setting
   733→standards, and scaling techniques to handle complex models and behaviors and expand to
   734→domains such as vision and reinforcement learning. Mechanistic interpretability could help
   735→prevent catastrophic outcomes as AI systems become more powerful and inscrutable. For
   736→an HTML version of the paper, visit https://leonardbereska.github.io/blog/2024/
   737→mechinterpreview/.
   738→1
   739→Introduction
   740→As AI systems rapidly become more sophisticated and general (Bubeck et al., 2023; Bengio et al., 2023),
   741→advancing our understanding of these systems is crucial to ensure their alignment (Ji et al., 2024) with
   742→human values and avoid catastrophic outcomes (Hendrycks et al., 2023; Hendrycks & Mazeika, 2022). The
   743→field of interpretability aims to demystify the internal processes of AI models, moving beyond evaluating
   744→performance alone. This review focuses on mechanistic interpretability, an emerging approach within the
   745→broader interpretability landscape that strives to comprehensively specify the computations underlying deep
   746→neural networks. We emphasize that understanding and interpreting these complex systems is not merely
   747→an academic endeavor – it’s a societal imperative to ensure AI remains trustworthy and beneficial.
   748→The interpretability landscape is undergoing a paradigm shift akin to the evolution from behaviorism to
   749→cognitive neuroscience in psychology. Historically, lacking tools for introspection, psychology treated the
   750→mind as a black box, focusing solely on observable behaviors. Similarly, interpretability has predominantly
   751→relied on black-box techniques (Casper et al., 2024), analyzing models based on input-output relationships or
   752→using attribution methods that, while probing deeper, still neglect the model’s internal architecture. However,
   753→just as advancements in neuroscience allowed for a deeper understanding of internal cognitive processes, the
   754→field of interpretability is now moving towards a more granula
   755→

content.text:
Here are the summaries for all 12 papers:

---

**1. Chen et al. (2024) -- "States Hidden in Hidden States: LLMs Emerge Discrete State Representations Implicitly"**
- **Authors:** Junhao Chen, Shengding Hu, Zhiyuan Liu, Maosong Sun (Tsinghua University)
- **Key contribution:** Discovers that LLMs form Implicit Discrete State Representations (IDSRs) within hidden states to perform multi-step arithmetic (e.g., adding 15 two-digit numbers) without chain-of-thought. They confirm IDSRs exist, trace their formation across layers/digits/sequences, and show models use them to produce answers -- though representations are lossy in current open-source models.
- **Relevance:** Directly relevant. Demonstrates that transformers encode discrete numeric state information in hidden states during computation -- a close analog to how interval boundaries might be stored internally.

---

**2. AlQuBoj et al. (2025) -- "Number Representations in LLMs: A Computational Parallel to Human Perception"**
- **Authors:** H. V. AlQuBoj, Hilal AlQuabeh, Velibor Bojkovic, Tatsuya Hiraoka, et al. (MBZUAI, Tohoku University, RIKEN)
- **Key contribution:** Shows that LLMs encode numbers on a logarithmic (sublinear) scale, mirroring the human "mental number line." Using PCA/PLS and geometric regression on hidden embeddings, they find compressed, non-uniform numerical spacing.
- **Relevance:** Highly relevant. The logarithmic internal number representation directly affects how interval boundaries would be encoded -- intervals near zero would have finer resolution than those at larger magnitudes.

---

**3. Yu & Ananiadou (2024) -- "Interpreting Arithmetic Mechanism in Large Language Models through Comparative Neuron Analysis"**
- **Authors:** Zeping Yu, Sophia Ananiadou (University of Manchester)
- **Key contribution:** Introduces Comparative Neuron Analysis (CNA) to trace a four-stage internal logic chain for arithmetic: feature enhancing (shallow FFN), feature transferring (shallow attention), feature predicting (arithmetic heads), and prediction enhancing (deep FFN). Identifies specialized attention heads and human-interpretable FFN neurons for arithmetic.
- **Relevance:** Highly relevant. The mechanistic dissection of how arithmetic is computed layer-by-layer provides a methodological template for investigating how interval membership (a comparison/arithmetic operation) might be computed internally.

---

**4. Bertolazzi et al. (2025) -- "The Validation Gap: A Mechanistic Analysis of How Language Models Compute Arithmetic but Fail to Validate It"**
- **Authors:** Leonardo Bertolazzi, Philipp Mondorf, Barbara Plank, Raffaella Bernardi (Trento, LMU Munich, Bozen-Bolzano)
- **Key contribution:** Through circuit analysis, discovers a structural dissociation: arithmetic computation occurs in higher layers while validation (error detection) happens in middle layers -- before arithmetic results are fully encoded. Identifies "consistency heads" that check surface-level numerical alignment.
- **Relevance:** Relevant. The finding that arithmetic circuits are localized in specific layers and structurally separated from validation circuits informs where to look for interval-checking mechanisms and suggests interval boundary comparisons may also be layer-stratified.

---

**5. Deng et al. (2024) -- "Language Models are Symbolic Learners in Arithmetic"**
- **Authors:** Chunyuan Deng, Zhiqi Li, Roy Xie, Ruidi Chang, Hanjie Chen (Rice, Georgia Tech, Duke)
- **Key contribution:** Argues LMs learn arithmetic via greedy symbolic shortcuts (pattern matching) rather than true algorithms. Introduces "subgroup induction" (adapted from Solomonoff Induction) showing a U-shaped accuracy pattern in multi-digit multiplication that mirrors shortcut quality -- LMs learn hierarchically from simple to complex symbol mappings.
- **Relevance:** Moderately relevant. If transformers store intervals via symbolic shortcuts rather than genuine boundary comparisons, this would fundamentally change the expected internal representation. Important for hypothesis formation about whether interval storage is algorithmic or pattern-based.

---

**6. Hasani et al. (2025) -- "Understanding Counting Mechanisms in Large Language and Vision-Language Models"**
- **Authors:** Hosein Hasani, Amirmohammad Izadi, Fatemeh Askari, et al. (Sharif University of Technology)
- **Key contribution:** Uses causal mediation and activation patching (via their "CountScope" tool) to reveal that individual tokens encode latent positional count information. Lower layers encode small counts; higher layers encode larger ones. An internal counter mechanism updates per item, stored in the final token, and is transferable between contexts.
- **Relevance:** Relevant. The progressive, layerwise emergence of numerical representations and the internal counter mechanism may parallel how interval boundaries are incrementally computed and stored across layers.

---

**7. Chang & Bisk (2024) -- "Language Models Need Inductive Biases to Count Inductively"**
- **Authors:** Yingshan Chang, Yonatan Bisk (Carnegie Mellon University)
- **Key contribution:** Extensive empirical study showing transformers cannot generalize counting inductively (out-of-distribution) without positional embeddings, unlike traditional RNNs. Tests RNNs, Transformers, State-Space Models, and RWKV. Highlights that transformers rely on positional embeddings as a crutch for counting.
- **Relevance:** Moderately relevant. Interval membership requires comparing a value against boundaries, which involves implicit counting/ordering. The finding that transformers depend on positional encodings for count generalization suggests similar limitations may apply to interval boundary generalization.

---

**8. Zhang, Cao & You (2024) -- "Counting Ability of Large Language Models and Impact of Tokenization"**
- **Authors:** Xiang Zhang, Juntai Cao, Chenyu You (UBC, Stony Brook)
- **Key contribution:** Investigates how BPE tokenization impacts LLM counting ability. Transformers are limited to TC0 complexity, making deep counting theoretically difficult. Tokenization choices cause substantial performance variation; character-level tokenization (used in expert models) differs fundamentally from BPE.
- **Relevance:** Moderately relevant. Tokenization determines how integer values are represented at the input level, which directly affects how interval boundaries can be internally encoded and compared. Important for experimental design choices.

---

**9. Fu et al. (2024) -- "Why Do Large Language Models (LLMs) Struggle to Count Letters?"**
- **Authors:** Tairan Fu, Raquel Ferrando, Javier Conde, Carlos Arriaga, Pedro Reviriego (Nanjing U. of Aeronautics, UPM Madrid)
- **Key contribution:** Systematic study showing LLMs can recognize letters but fail to count them. Errors correlate strongly with counting complexity (number of letters with count > 1) rather than word/token frequency. Models fail when letters appear more than twice.
- **Relevance:** Low-to-moderate relevance. Primarily about character counting rather than numeric intervals, but the finding that complexity of the counting operation (not memorization) drives errors is informative for understanding limitations in numeric processing tasks.

---

**10. Rahman & Mishra (2025) -- "A Fragile Number Sense: Probing the Elemental Limits of Numerical Reasoning in LLMs"**
- **Authors:** Roussel Rahman, Aashwin Ananda Mishra (Stanford, SLAC)
- **Key contribution:** Probes LLM numerical reasoning across escalating complexity: basic arithmetic, advanced operations, primality checking, and Game of 24. Models succeed at deterministic algorithmic tasks but fail at combinatorial search, suggesting proficiency is algorithm recall/pattern matching rather than flexible reasoning.
- **Relevance:** Moderately relevant. Establishes that LLM "number sense" is fragile and pattern-based, which bears on whether transformers can robustly represent interval membership (a deterministic operation) versus more complex numeric reasoning.

---

**11. Gross et al. (2024) -- "Compact Proofs of Model Performance via Mechanistic Interpretability"**
- **Authors:** Jason Gross, Rajashree Agrawal, Thomas Kwa, Euan Ong, Chun Hei Yip, Alex Gibson, Soufiane Noubir, Lawrence Chan
- **Key contribution:** Uses mechanistic interpretability to derive formal accuracy lower bounds for a small transformer trained on Max-of-K. Tests 102 proof strategies across 151 seeds and 4 values of K. Finds shorter proofs require deeper mechanistic understanding, and more faithful understanding yields tighter bounds.
- **Relevance:** Highly relevant. Max-of-K is a comparison/ordering task closely related to interval membership (which requires comparing a value against two boundaries). The formal verification methodology could be adapted to prove properties about interval-storing circuits.

---

**12. Bereska & Gavves (2024) -- "Mechanistic Interpretability for AI Safety -- A Review"**
- **Authors:** Leonard Bereska, Efstratios Gavves (University of Amsterdam)
- **Key contribution:** Comprehensive review of mechanistic interpretability: reverse engineering neural network computations into human-understandable algorithms. Covers features, representations, causal dissection methodologies, scalability challenges, and connections to AI safety (alignment, control, dual-use risks).
- **Relevance:** Highly relevant as methodological background. Provides the conceptual and technical framework (features, circuits, causal interventions) that would be used to investigate how transformers internally represent and compute interval membership.
